coredata.dc:title: Waterborne fluorescent dual anti-counterfeiting ink based on Yb/Er-carbon quantum dots grafted with dialdehyde nano-fibrillated cellulose

coredata.dc:description: Nanofibrillated cellulose (NFC) is becoming popular in the field of anti-counterfeiting material due to its favorable biocompatibility, renewability, and easy modification properties, which give it great potentials as carrier of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Herein, we report an effective method to fabricate Yb and Er doped CQDs grafted onto dialdehyde NFC (DANFC). Owning to special rheological properties of NFC, a waterborne fluorescent dual anti-counterfeiting ink was rationally designed and successfully prepared by adding NFC to waterborne ink to form a stable network structure and increase the thixotropy and yield stress. The resulting CQDs exhibited both photoluminescence (PL) and up-conversion luminescence (UCPL), emitting blue and green fluorescence at excitation wavelengths of 370 and 980 nm, respectively. The study provides a novel method to prepare the waterborne fluorescent dual anti-counterfeiting ink based on Yb and Er doped CQDs/DANFC composites, which provides a reference for its application in printing and packaging industry.

head.ce:title: Waterborne fluorescent dual anti-counterfeiting ink based on Yb/Er-carbon quantum dots grafted with dialdehyde nano-fibrillated cellulose

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Plant Fiber Research Center, State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering, School of Light Industry and Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, 510640, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering
School of Light Industry and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Plant Fiber Research Center, State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering, School of Light Industry and Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, P.R. China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple way to fabricate novel and simple method to prepare the waterborne fluorescent dual anti-counterfeiting ink based on Yb and Er doped CQDs/DANFC composites, which provides a reference for its anti-counterfeiting application in printing and packaging industry.

Nanofibrillated cellulose (NFC) is becoming popular in the field of anti-counterfeiting material due to its favorable biocompatibility, renewability, and easy modification properties, which give it great potentials as carrier of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Herein, we report an effective method to fabricate Yb and Er doped CQDs grafted onto dialdehyde NFC (DANFC). Owning to special rheological properties of NFC, a waterborne fluorescent dual anti-counterfeiting ink was rationally designed and successfully prepared by adding NFC to waterborne ink to form a stable network structure and increase the thixotropy and yield stress. The resulting CQDs exhibited both photoluminescence (PL) and up-conversion luminescence (UCPL), emitting blue and green fluorescence at excitation wavelengths of 370 and 980 nm, respectively. The study provides a novel method to prepare the waterborne fluorescent dual anti-counterfeiting ink based on Yb and Er doped CQDs/DANFC composites, which provides a reference for its application in printing and packaging industry.

ce:section.ce:para: In the information age, data security is particularly important in economic and military fields as well as in our daily lives (Cui, Hegde, Phang, Lee, & Ling, 2014). To ensure the safety and integrity of important information (such as banknotes, documents, certificates, brands, etc.), anti-counterfeit technology is increasingly demanded as they can produce sensitive and stable responses in special forms (Yoon et al., 2013). In recent years, several types of anti-counterfeiting mechanisms have been developed, such as marking (Hu, Chen, Tang, Hu, & Zhou, 2012; Hu, Zhong, Chen, & Chen, 2014), bar code (Yang et al., 2017), thermal imaging, and fluorescence detection (Jiang et al., 2016; Liang et al., 2019; Nair, Abhilash, & Surendran, 2019; Yin et al., 2019). Among them, fluorescent ink has emerged as candidates with high potentials, since other materials either suffer from preparation complexity, low stability, or a lack of multiple anti-counterfeiting capabilities (Bu et al., 2019; Chen, Hu et al., 2019, Chen, Xie et al., 2019; Kumar, Singh, & Gupta, 2016; Ma et al., 2019; Qu, Wang, Lu, Liu, & Wang, 2012; Song, Lin et al., 2016; Shahla, Davood, Omid, & Masoud, 2020; Xu, Zhang et al., 2019, Xu, Lei et al., 2019). Anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink has the advantages of simple preparation, convenient use, and remarkable effect, so it is widely used in many aspects of life. Generally, traditional luminescent materials including rare earth metals (Cuevas et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2018), CdS (Tang et al., 2016), ZnS (Prasad & Karthikeyan, 2019), CdTe (Jiang, Zhu, Yao, Fu, & Guan, 2012), photochromic compounds (Yang, Ho, & Chan, 2019), are controlled by monochromic radiation under ultraviolet (UV) and near infrared (NIR) light excitation. However, the photocontrol of such compounds is still very challenging, because the fluorescent substances usually have unfavorable features such as inflexible adjustability, single color emission, and low color purity. Compared with traditional luminescent materials, the new luminescent materials can be smartly controlled by excitable wavelengths and have multiple emission modes exhibiting improved photophysical properties, facile tunability, and excellent compatibility, which are considered to be effective ways for anti-counterfeiting (Chen, Xie et al., 2019; Yao, Tian, & Wu, 2019; Zhang et al., 2018).

In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new comer of 0 D dimension carbon nanomaterials (Esteves da Silva & Gonçalves, 2011; Jing, Zhao, Sun, Zhong, & Peng, 2019; Sun et al., 2006; Wang et al., 2018), have excellent physiochemical properties, such as high optical stability (Kelarakis, 2015), low-toxicity (Jiang et al., 2016), super conductivity (Zhao et al., 2019), good biocompatibility (Guo et al., 2017), simple preparation, and high quantum yield. CQDs are widely used in fluorescence anti-counterfeiting, bioimaging (Demir et al., 2018), drug delivery (Feng, Ai, An, Yang, & Zhao, 2016), photocatalysis (Xu, Bao, Zhou, Zeng, & Hu, 2016), sensor probes (Ma et al., 2019), supercapacitors (Zhao et al., 2019), and so on. These excellent physiochemical properties make CQDs a promising material in many technological areas (Li et al., 2018; You, Zhang, Liu, & Lei, 2016). However, the poor production yield (Jing et al., 2019), and single luminescence behavior (Zhu et al., 2013) have limited their practical applications. Many studies have shown that doping heteroatoms into CQDs is an effective way to increase quantum yield and improve the fluorescence properties of CQDs for specific applications (Zhao et al., 2019). Yang et al. prepared La-doped CQDs (La-CQDs) through microwave pyrolysis, and the quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime are greatly improved (Yang et al., 2018). Similarly, Zhao et al. described Yb3+-doped CQDs though one step hydrothermal method, achieving the effect of NIR and visible dual emission (Zhang et al., 2018). The feasibility of anti-counterfeiting performance is normally controlled by UV and NIR double excitation via blending some rare earth elements such as Yb3+ and Er3+ with CQDs (Gayathri, Ghosh, Sudhakara, & Viswanath, 2015; Xue et al., 2018). Er3+ ion has a specific UCPL performance because of its large stokes shift and line-shaped emission in the near infrared region. Therefore, it is interesting to dope Er3+ into CQDs to give them more complex luminescence behavior. Meanwhile, it is necessary to embed the use of sensitizers to luminescence of Er3+ ions as it can generate energy transfer between the sensitizer and the activator to produce efficient fluorescence emission. Yb3+ ion is a good choice to introduce the sensitizer because its absorption cross section near 980 nm which can well match the energy difference of Er3+, and resulting in higher probability of transferring energy to enhance UCPL (Shi et al., 2018; Wei, Zhang, Liu, Han, & Yuan, 2017; Zhang et al., 2018). However, the relationships between the CQDs/substrate interface photon energy transfer have not been systemically studied, and the mechanism of UV and NIR double excitation by CQDs based composites still needs to be further studied.

Nanofibrillated cellulose (NFC), a rod-like cellulosic fiber with a diameter of tens of nanometers and a length of several microns, has aroused much interest due to its larger specific surface area, special rheological properties, a large number of hydroxyl groups, higher mechanical modulus, etc. (Cao et al., 2019; Deepa et al., 2015; Khalil et al., 2014; Oksman et al., 2016; Qi, Cheng, Ye, Zhu, & Aparicio, 2019; Trache, Hussin, Haafiz, & Thakur, 2017). More importantly, we found that NFC has a similar rheological properties and thixotropic properties with conventional waterborne ink (Cao et al., 2019; Qi et al., 2019). When a shear force is applied to the solution, the viscosity drops rapidly to a lower level, but the viscosity would return to the original level once the shear force removed, which is completely consistent with the pseudoplastic fluid (Quraishi, Plappert, Grießer, Gindl-Altmutter, & Liebner, 2019; Wang & Fu, 2019). Considering the advantages of CQDs and NFC, the interface barrier can be eliminated, and the nanoscaled mixtures with intimate contacts can be fabricated. In addition, we expected to improve the accessible area between CQDs and NFC interface and then form a channel for photon energy transfer. And also, to construct the system of anti-counterfeiting waterborne ink by adding NFC for the improvement of the overall rheological properties.

In this paper, the waterborne fluorescent dual anti-counterfeiting ink based on UV and NIR double excitation mechanism was successfully prepared. CQDs were doped with rare earth elements Yb and Er, and successfully grafted onto NFC by reductive amination reaction. Then, NFC was added into waterborne ink because of its special rheological properties. Thixotropy and curing properties of waterborne ink were measured. The results showed that NFC, as a carrier of CQDs can be added to waterborne ink, which is of great significance in improving the rheological properties of ink.

The cellulose sample (pine pulp, DP = 1000) was supplied by Hubei Chemical Fiber Co., Ltd. (Hubei, China). Commercial endoglucanase (Banzyme 2900) was purchased from UPM-kymmene Co., Ltd (Jiangsu, China). Ytterbium chloride hexahydrate (YbCl3·6H2O, purity ≥ 99.99 %) and erbium chloride hexahydrate (ErCl3·6H2O, purity ≥ 99.9 %) were purchased from Shanghai Sheeny Metal Material Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 2-picolineborane (C6H10NB, purity ≥ 95 %) and polyvinyl alcohol 1799 [PVA-1799, alcoholysis degree 98–99 % (mol/mol)] were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St Louis, MO, USA). Citric acid (CA, purity ≥ 99 %), sodium periodate (NaIO4, purity ≥ 99 %), ethanediol (purity ≥ 98 %), ethylenediamine (EDA, purity ≥ 99 %), and hydroxylammonium chloride (NH2OH·HCl, purity ≥ 98.5 %) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and all chemicals were used as received without further purification. Deionized water was used for the dissolution of reagents (Milli-Q, 18.2 MΩ cm at 25 °C, Burlington, MA, USA).

NFC was prepared by two-stage enzymatic hydrolysis using cellulose endonuclease. Absolutely dried cellulose (70 g) was added to 1000 mL distilled water and soaked for 12 h. Subsequently, the fibers were degraded for 1 h with a trough beater. 5 % citric acid-NaOH buffer (pH = 5.25) was added to the slurry for 2 h. For the first time, cellulose endonuclease was added to the pulp for 2 h, and the pulp was ground 20 times with a mechanical mill (scale-5 mm). For the second time, cellulose endonuclease was added to the slurry for 1.5 h. The slurry was ground 10 times with a mechanical mill (scale-10 mm). The pulp sample was homogenized 5 times by homogenizer, and the slurry was collected and defined as NFC.

In order to provide a functional group bonding position for the combination of CQDs, NFC was oxidized by periodate to prepare dialdehyde NFC. The vicinal hydroxyl groups at positions C2 and C3 of NFC were partially oxidized to aldehyde groups during the periodate oxidation procedure. In brief, NaIO4 (3.02 g) was dissolved in a NFC suspension (300 mL, 0.72 wt %). The mixture was stirred gently at 65 °C for 3 h in the dark and was terminated by adding 100 mL glycol. Then the mixture was centrifuged for many times to remove excess chemicals. The centrifugal precipitate was recovered and defined as DANFC (41.8 % yield from NFC).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Yb/Er doped amino-functionalized CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of CQDs involved the hydrothermal treatment of carbon source. The required amount of citric acid (1.5 g), ethanediamine (1.26 g), YbCl3·6H2O (0.3 g), and ErCl3·6H2O (0.03 g) were added to distilled water (10 mL) to form a transparent solution. After stirring for 5 min, the mixture was added to a stainless-steel autoclave with a volume of 50 mL. The autoclave was heated in a furnace at 150 ℃ for 5 h, and then cooled to room temperature. The obtained transparent pale yellow liquid was then subjected to dialysis (200 molecular-weight cut off) for about 2 days to obtain the doped CQDs.

The combination of DANFC and Yb/Er-CQDs was realized by reductive amination. It was carried out on the basis of previous method with a minor modification. First, a fivefold excess of amine in relation to the aldehyde groups of DNFAC was mixed with deionized water, and the pH of the solution was set to 4.5 with sodium acetate buffer (self-made, 18 g sodium acetate mixed with 9.8 mL glacial acetic acid and added deionized water to 1000 mL at constant volume). Non-dried DANFC and a twofold excess of 2-picoline borane, based on the assessed amount of the aldehyde groups, were added to the suspension, and the reaction was continued for 24 h with magnetic stirring in a closed container at room temperature. The mixture was then dialyzed and centrifuged to remove the remaining Yb/Er-CQDs and byproducts until the washing raffinate was colorless and without any fluorescence under ultraviolet radiation.

Waterborne ink formulation: PVA as binder (mass fraction 4 %), defoamer (purchase, mass fraction 0.5 %), sodium fatty acid (mass fraction 1 %), urea (mass fraction 1 %), and distilled water. The viscosity of the ink was 0.12 Pa.s, which conforms to the reasonable range of the viscosity of flexible waterborne ink. Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC nanocomposites (1.0 wt.%) was slowly added to PVA waterborne ink. The mixed solution was further gently stirred at 45 °C for 0.5 h. Finally, the mixed ink was obtained as Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC-PVA hybrids.

FTIR spectra (from 400 to 4000 cm−1) were recorded on a Bruker VERTEX 70 spectro- photometer at a resolution of 2 cm−1 by the KBr-disk method. UV–vis absorption spectra were performed on a Hach DR6000 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hach, Colorado, USA) in the range of 200−900 nm in 1 cm quartz cuvette. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra measurements were conducted using a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer instrument apparatus. Morphology of NFC and CQDs were examined by atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker, Germany) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100 F). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded with an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (AXIS Ultra DLD, Kratos Co., England) with mono Al Kα (1486.6 eV) as the X-ray source. Leica TCS-SP5 confocal laser scanning microscopy (Leica microsystems CMS GmbH, Manheim, Germany) was used to research the fluorescence of Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC nanomaterials.

The crystallinity properties of nanocomposite were taken on a D8 ADVANCE diffractometer (Bruker, Germany), with diffraction angles (2θ) ranging from 5 to 60°. The data processing procedures were executed with the Jade 6.0 program. The crystallinity index (CI, %) of NFC and nanocomposites was calculated using the following equation (Cheng et al., 2019):
CI (%) = [(I002 - Iam)/I002]×100
where I200 is the maximum intensity of the principal peak at 2θ = 22.6° corresponding to the plane (002), and Iam is the intensity of diffraction attributed to the amorphous phase 2θ = 18°.

Aldehyde content (AC) was measured according to hydroxylamine hydrochloride titration method (Chen et al., 2018; Tran, Makuvaza, Munson, & Bennett, 2017). The reaction principle is that dialdehyde groups are transformed to oximes by reacting with hydroxylamine hydrochloride. In brief, 0.4 g DATNFC was added to hydroxylamine hydrochloride (15 mL, 0.25 mol/L, pH 3.01) to perform the reaction at room temperature for 36 h. Subsequently, the contents of aldehyde groups in DATNFC were calculated by titrating with 0.05 mol/L of NaOH until pH reached to 3.01. The reaction principle and calculation formula are as follows:
−CHO + NH2OH·HCl → −CHNOH + HCl + H2O
HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H2O
AC = CV/m (mmol/g)
where AC is the content of the aldehyde groups of samples, mmol/g; C is normality of NaOH (0.05 mol/L); V is the consumed volume of NaOH solution, mL; and m is the weight of oven-dried samples, g.

The quantum yield (QY) of CQDs was calculated using quinine sulfate (Φst = 0.54) as a reference standard. The calculation formula was as follows (Jing et al., 2019):
Φs= Φst (Ms/M st)(K st/Ks)(ηs 2/η st 2)
where the subscript “s” represents the samples. “st” is the reference compound. The Φ is the fluorescence quantum yield; M is the integrated fluorescence intensity measured by fluorescence emission spectrum. K is the absorbance under the excitation wavelength. η are the refractive indices of the solvents, which are both approximately 1.33. To minimize reabsorption effects, the absorbance of all solution lies in the range of 0.02−0.1.

Rheological measurements were performed using a rotational rheometer (DHR-2 -TA Instruments). The experiments have been performed using a parallel plate geometry (PP25−40 mm, peltier plate, aluminium) with a gap set at 1 mm. A pre-shear operation (shear rate = 1 s−1– 60 s) was applied before the formal test to keep the initial state of the suspension consistent. A specific three steps program was adopted by Fanny Hoeng et al. (Hoeng, Denneulin, Reverdy-Bruas, Krosnicki, & Bras, 2017). Firstly, a low shear rate have been applied (2 s−1–60 s) to simulate the ink at rest on the screen. Secondly, the ink was applied a high shear (1000 s−1–15 s) to measure the recovery rate of the structural damage of the suspension. Finally, a new low shear rate period (2s−1–150 s) representative of the ink restructuration after the printing.

The yield stress and curing property of ink was measured by Oscillating Test. Before the dynamic viscoelastic measurements, the linear viscoelastic region (LVE) was determined by strain sweep tests for all dispersions by applying strain sweeps. The viscosity-temperature characteristics of ink was measured to represent characterization of storage and service performance.

The fabrication of Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC was shown in Scheme 1 . Firstly, the double rare-doped CQDs (Yb/Er-CQDs) with amino groups (proved by FTIR, Fig. 2f) with the average size of 3.42 nm (Fig. 1 f) were first synthesized through hydrothermal method using CA, EDA, YbCl3, and ErCl3 as raw materials, respectively. The amide bonds were prepared by dehydration condensation reaction between carboxyl group and amino group of CA, and the coordinate bonds were generated under the attraction of positive and negative charges by Yb3+ ions and carboxyl functional groups. More information on the chemical composition and uniformity of Yb/Er- CQDs-DANFC can be founded in Fig. S4 from Supporting Information, thereinto the carbon, oxygen, ytterbium, and erbium are evenly distributed on the Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC. After Yb/Er doping, the Yb/Er-CQDs shows the strong photoluminescence under UV irradiation (the inset of Fig. S1 from SI), and also exhibiting the typical PL behavior (Fig. 4). Simultaneously, NFC was oxidized by periodate to provide a functional bonding position for the combination of CQDs. The cellulose oxidation leads to cleavage of the C2-C3 bonds in the unit of β-d-glucopyranoside and whereafter generates two aldehyde functional groups on the C2 and C3 positions (Li et al., 2019). Subsequently, the amine groups of Yb/Er-CQDs interact with the aldehyde group of DANFC to form an imine bond, which was then reduced to stabilize CN bond by using 2-picoline borane. Hence, Yb/Er-CQDs were successfully decorated onto the surface of aldehyde-based NFC.

The micro-structures of the CQDs and DANFC were investigated by AFM and TEM. Fig. 1 a shows the typical AFM image of the NFC, exhibiting an average size of 52.83 nm in diameter and several micrometers in length. Thus, it gives rise to an aspect ratio around 6.5–26.5. TEM image (Fig. 1b) of the NFC reveals that numerous spherical CQDs with the size of about 5 nm are uniformly dispersed on the surface of DANFC. The particle size of CQDs was further proved by Fig. 1 c and f. High-resolution TEM image (HR-TEM) (Fig. 1d) clearly exhibits the high crystallinity of the CQDs and the lattice spacing of 0.18 nm was correspond to the (111) crystal plane of CQDs according to the facet of graphitic carbon (Jing et al., 2019). AFM image (Fig. S2) further reveals that the CQDs are quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles. The typical topographic height of CQDs is 1–3 nm (Fig. S3). The above results demonstrated that the CQDs were successfully doped on the surface of DANFC. Furthermore, elemental maps of EDS (Fig. S4) show that C, O, N, Yb, and Er are evenly distributed on the Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC materials.

The wide-angle X-ray diffraction (WAXRD) patterns was used to show the purity and crystal structure of the as-prepared NFC, DATNFC, DATNFC/CQDs, and Yb/Er-DANFC/CQDs (Fig. 2a). The XRD patterns peak for CQDs at 2θ = 19∼22° is clearly observed in Fig. 2b, revealing an amorphous carbon phase and abundant defects at the edges of CQDs (Jing et al., 2019; Sahu, Behera, Maiti, & Mohapatra, 2012). The WAXRD patterns of DANFC and the DANFC/CQDs nanohybrids display diffraction peaks at 2θ = 15.07° (101), 22.52° (002), and 34.12° (040), which correspond to the typical cellulose I crystalline form. The diffraction patterns of the doped nanohybrids are consistent with the Work Outcomes of Peers (Zhang et al., 2018). As compared to DATNFC, the diffracted intensities of the (002) in DANFC/CQDs and Yb/Er-DANFC/CQDs were clearly strengthened. This was due to the fact that the typical diffraction peak region of CQDs coincided with the typical diffraction region of nanocellulose (002), indicating that homogeneous CQDs was embedded in the DANFC matrix. In addition, DANFC exhibited lower crystallinity (41.56) than that of NFC (63.71), ascribed to the destruction of ordered crystal structures during NaIO4 oxidation (Lu, Yu, Zhou, & Yao, 2016). A cleavage of glucopyranose rings occurred after NaIO4 oxidation, resulting in a lower degree of crystallinity of DANFC. The low crystallinity was expected to form a continuous flow system that acted as a non-newtonian fluid, thus contributing to form the entangled network in the ink system. Moreover, the aldehyde content showed the expected increase in DATNFC with further oxidation with sodium periodate (before, 0.035 mmol/g; after, 4.021 mmol/g). XPS was performed to investigate the chemical composition and bonding information on Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC and CQDs, as shown in Fig. 2 and S5. Fig. 2 c shows the fully scanned spectra of Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC nanocomposites, reveal that the as-prepared nanocomposites mainly contain chemical element. The XPS survey spectra of CQDs (Fig. S5a) shows an obvious peak of C 1s at ∼284.0 eV, a peak of O 1s at ∼531.3 eV, and a peak of N 1s at ∼397.4 eV, respectively. The high-resolution spectra of C 1s exhibit four main peaks in Fig. S5b. The binding energy peak at 284.1 eV confirms the presence of graphitic or aliphatic structure (CC and CC). The peak at ∼286.8 eV suggests the presence of CO, and the peak at 285.2 eV can be assigned to CO and CN. This indicates that the successful synthesis of CQDs using citric acid as carbon source. The XPS spectrum in Fig. 2 c reveals that the Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC are mainly composed of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, ytterbium, and erbium elements. For Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC, the content of carbon and oxygen account for 50.84 % and 35.19 %, respectively, indicating that carbon and oxygen were the main components of CQDs. Yb and Er elements are successfully doped into CQDs with Yb content of 3.19 % and Er content of 0.35 %. Fig. 2d, e illustrate the curve fitting of the high-resolution multiplex scan spectra of the C 1s region and N 1s region for Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC nanocomposites. As shown in Fig. 2d, the binding energy with peaks at 281.48, 283.04, and 284.29 eV are attributed to carbon structure of CC/CC, CO/CN, and CO, respectively. And in Fig. 2a, Yb/Er-CQDs contains free amino group, while N 1 s disappears in Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC, which is consistent with the results of infrared spectroscopy. It proves that CQDs has been successfully grafted onto DANFC. Fig. 2 f shows the FTIR spectra of CQDs, Yb/Er-CQDs, and Yb/Er-DANFC-CQDs nano-composites. The band at 1630 cm−1 was attributed to CC vibration whereas the peak at 3560 cm−1 and 3392 cm−1 were associated with primary amine groups. The results suggested that amino groups were loaded on the surface of the CQDs. The characteristic absorption band at 3435 cm−1, 2914 cm−1, and 1730 cm−1 were ascribed to OH stretching vibrations, symmetric CH vibrations, and the stretching vibration of CO (Li et al., 2019; Sun, Hou, Liu, & Ni, 2015), respectively, revealing that there were aldehyde functional groups on the surface of the DANFC. The aldehyde structure enables DANFC to be more easily modified with other functional nanoparticles, such as CQDs. The band at 1063 cm−1 was corresponded to the stretching vibration of CN, showing that CQDs were successfully grafted onto the surface of NFC. Based on the Raman analysis (SI), it can be found that the Raman spectra (Fig. S6, from SI) exhibit two broad bands at about 1367 cm−1 (D band) and 1578 cm−1 (G band), corresponding to the sp3 and sp2 hybrid carbons, respectively.

In the UV–vis spectra (Fig. S1), the synthesized CODs showed a broad absorption peak around 220–420 nm, the signal for as-prepared CODs revealed broad absorption in the visible light region due to pronounced interactions between the aromatic π systems. And a strong absorption peak at 241 nm and 348 nm could be clearly observed, the former is mainly attributed to the transition from π to π* of CC, while the latter is due to the transition from n to π* of the CO bond, which is similar to previous reports (Liang, Ma, Shi, Li, & Yang, 2013). The CQDs’ solutions show no obvious fluorescent emission when they are excited around 250 nm, which indicates that the inherent aromatic π system is not the efficient emission center (Zeng et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2019). Furthermore, the property of broad absorption in the visible light can help to build a multi-level auxiliary anti-counterfeiting system. The two pictures of the CODs aqueous solution (the inset of Fig. S1) displayed different color under vis light and UV light, where the blue fluorescence of CODs can be clearly observed. It is generally known that photoluminescence is one of the most outstanding feature of the quantum dots. To further study the optical properties of the CQDs, PL spectra were carried out in detail, as shown in the Fig. 3
. The results showed that the PL spectra of CQDs depended on the excitation wavelength.

The emission peak gradually shifts to a longer wavelength with the increasing excitation wavelength, suggesting that the CQDs showed an excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior. When the excitation wavelength increased from 370 to 440 nm, the peak of emission spectrum shifts from 440 to 490 nm (Fig. 3). The excitation wavelengths might affect the subsequent excited-state energy distribution, the charge separation, and/or the confinement of electrons and holes on the CQDs’ surface and their radiative recombination (Sahu et al., 2012). This narrow photoluminescence emission region (440−490 nm) confirms that the size distribution of CQDs is relatively narrow, because there are similar quantum effects and emission traps on the surface (Park et al., 2014), which is consistent with the results of TEM. The CQDs solution (1 mL) were subsequently diluted 40 times, 100 times, 200 times, and 500 times, and the PL spectra of CQDs also shows a concentration-dependent behavior. As increasing the dilution factor of the carbon point, the emission peaks shifted to a longer wavelength. As previously reported on the CQDs, the PL property of CQDs was mainly due to the presence of large number of OH and CO groups on the surface. Noted that these bonds made many CQDs gather together with a high polarity. These polarity group cause the excitation wavelength dependence of carbon point in the long wavelength range (Ding, Yu, Wei, & Xiong, 2016; Khan, Gupta, Verma, & Nandi, 2015; Zhu et al., 2013).

Fig. 4 c depicts the corresponding CIE chromaticity coordinates in chromaticity maps, and the tunable luminescence colors were recorded by CIE from A'point (x = 0.15, y = 0.10) to K'(x = 0.27, y = 0.61). From the normalized fluorescence spectra of Yb and Er doped CQDs, it can be seen that the Yb/Er-CQDs still have the typical wavelength-dependent fluorescence properties. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of the modified CQDs at 370 nm has been greatly strengthened after Yb and Er intercalation, as shown in Fig. S7. More importantly, the fluorescence quantum yield of Yb/Er-CQDs increases significantly from 10 % to 28 % after Yb and Er doping, exhibiting stronger fluorescence properties. In comparison, the quantum yield of the isothiocyanat decorated cellulose nanocrystals prepared by a similar process is 30.7 %, similarly exhibiting high fluorescence brightness (Fan et al., 2019). The fluorescence spectra of Yb/Er-CQDs is obtained as shown in Fig. 4 a. The emission spectra of the composite carbon point peaked at 440 and 550 nm under the excitation of 370 nm and 980 nm external light, respectively, which indicate that the composite CQDs has both PL and UCPL properties. And the lighting effect picture is shown in Fig. S8. The emission spectrum of the undoped CQDs under 980 nm excitation in Fig. S9 shows that the UCPL is not a manifestation of the inherent properties of CQDs.

According to the previous reports on the CQDs, the luminescence mechanism CQDs and Yb/Er-CQDs should be summarized as follows. As shown in Fig. 4d, by using 370 nm wavelength light irradiation, CQDs transits from ground state to singlet state (1π-π*) with an energy of 25,000 cm−1. Generally, the emission band at 450 nm is considered to be the triplet level of CQDs (3π-π*). According to Reinhouldt’s empirical rule (Zhang et al., 2017), the energy gap △E (1π-π*–3π-π*) less than 5000 cm−1 would endow the energy transfer path ways as sketched (1LC) in Fig. 4 d. Subsequently, the first photon of irradiation (980 nm) elevates an electron to the excited state 2 F 5/2 level of Yb3+ ion, and the ion transfer the energy to the adjacent Er3+ ion then returns to the ground state. As a result, the energy transfer promote the Er3+ ion form the ground state 4 I 15/2 level evolves to the 4 I 11/2 state. Thereafter, the second 980 nm photon transits the Er3+ ion from 4 I 11/2 to 4 F 7/2 level by the energy transfer. The electron in the Er3+
4 F 7/2 state decays non-radiatively to a lower energy state of 4 S 3/2 through multiphoton relaxation, emits 550 nm green light through radiative transition from the 4 S 3/2 state to the ground state 4 I 15/2 ( 2 H 11/2 → 4 I 15/2 , 4 S 3/2 → 4 I 15/2 ).

As mentioned above, it can be seen that under the excitation light of 415 nm, DANFC-CQDs composites (green is to show a more pronounced color contrast) show strong blue fluorescence (Fig. 5 -2,3), whereas the DANFC sample has no fluorescence phenomenon under the excitation light (Fig. 5-1). Under the excitation of an external laser, surface imperfection and abundant polar groups (such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl groups) on the surface of the CQDs caused the luminescence behavior in the blue light region. The surface mapping showed a special branch-like morphology of Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC composites with a lot of fluorescent dots, and the uniform coloration which was identical to that in Fig. 1b, implying that Yb/Er-CQDs entered the DANFC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation on the rheological behavior of Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC ink
ce:section.ce:para: Yb/Er doped CQDs can be used as the invisible security ink because of their favorable fluorescence intensity and photo stability. In our study, the best-performing Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC should be well-dispersed in solvent for the sequential printing formability. It is well known that waterborne ink is a shear thinning non-Newtonian fluid showing the better printing adaptability. This is due to its low viscosity when suffered from high-speed stirring, but high viscosity at a low shear rate (Wang & Fu, 2019). We first prepared the waterborne ink by adding PVA, defoamer, sodium fatty acid, and urea, stirring for 2 h, and investigated the rheological behavior of Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC and PVA hybrids. For the PVA solution, its thixotropy was almost constant, because the viscosity was not influenced by changing shear rates (Fig. 6 a). While the viscosity of Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC and PVA mixtures initially increased, and they gradually decreased afterwards showing a certain shear thinning property (Fig. 6b). In fact, the temperature of ink is increased by the friction of ink roll during the process of printing transfer. The feature of thixotropy turns to be fluidity and ductility and therefore make it easy to transfer. As ink transferred onto the paper, the ink becomes thicker and thinner because of the loss of external force. Based on these properties, the ink will not overflow to the surrounding area, thus forming a good impression on the paper. Gratifyingly, from Fig. 6b, it can be seen that the viscosity of the mixed solution restored to the original level in a short time (about 10 s) as changing high shear to a lower shear, indicating that the addition of NFC accelerated the recovery of the structural damage of the ink and gave it a good thixotropic property.

As shown in Fig. 6c, the linear viscoelastic region (left side of red dashed line) is relatively wide, indicating that the mixed solution of Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC-PVA is homogeneous, because the wider the linear viscoelastic region, the stronger the interaction between the molecular chains in the solution system. The results of the small-frequency amplitude scanning was also performed to investigate the shear thinning ability of solution while the whole test process is in the linear viscoelastic region. As shown in Fig. 6d, the storage modulus and loss modulus increased with the change of angular frequency from 0.1 rad/s to 200 rad/s, whereas the complex viscosity decreased linearly, which could be explained by the addition of Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC that enhanced the shear thinning ability of Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC-PVA mixtures. Overall, the storage modulus of Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC-PVA mixtures showed a low dependence on frequency, and the loss modulus increased clearly with the increase of frequency. Moreover, the composite viscosity of the resulted mixed solution changed slowly from solid state to gel state, indicating that its viscoelasticity is suitable for large continuous inkjet printing. The frequency characteristics also have guiding significance for the working performance of inks in practical applications. For PVA hybrids, the relationship between shear stress and shear rate can be fitted by Bingham plastic model:
(1) σ = σo + η × γ
where σ is the shear stress, σo is the yield stress or yield point, η is the plastic viscosity, and γ is the shear rate. Based on the Bingham plastic model (Li, Wu, Song, Qing, & Wu, 2015) we can find that it is not a linear relationship between shear stress and shear rate during printing, especially at low shear rates. Therefore, Herschel-Bulkey model is used to simulate the mixed solution.
(2) σ = σo + k × γn
where k is the flow consistency coefficient, and n is the flow behavior index.

The curves in Fig. 7
a, b were fitted respectively, the results of variance were R2 5 = 0.999994 and R2 6 = 0.999250. The higher R2 value shows that the selection of the model is more appropriate. The fitting results showed that the shear stress increased proportionally in the range of shear rate from 0.1 1/s to 100 1/s, while the viscosity of PVA solution remained basically constant (Fig. 7 a, b). At this time the yield stress and viscosity were 7.737 × 10E-3 Pa and 0.1612 Pa.s, respectively. The Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC-PVA mixed solution showed a certain shear thinning property because its shear stress increased and simultaneously the viscosity decreased as increasing the shear rate. Interestingly, the shear-thinning curve in Fig. 7 b can be divided into two characteristic regions. In regions I, the nanofibers slowly oriented along the shear direction under the low shear force, causing a gradual decrease of viscosity. In region II, the downward trend of viscosity curve was slowing down and an evident plateau was observed. It was proposed that more entangled network was formed in this area, which prevented the continuous reduction of viscosity. Different shear-thinning behaviors of NFC suspensions were reported in previous literatures. For example, Mei-Chun et al. (Song, Wu et al., 2016) found the four-region shear-thinning behavior of NFC suspensions. Similarly, the other scholars discussed rheological properties of nanoscale cellulose aqueous suspension, and also found the appearance of Newtonian plateau. They confirmed the high network forming capacity of MFC water dispersions and quantitatively measured this network forming capacity in dynamic flow studies (Dai et al., 2019; Iotti, Gregersen, Moe, & Lenes, 2011; Nechyporchuk, Belgacem, & Pignon, 2014; Pääkkö et al., 2007). It is generally believed that the yield stress is the minimum required for ink to change from gel state to sol state. For PVA solution, too low yield stress σ0 will cause ink to change from gel state to sol state, resulting in ink accumulation, slow drying and unclear printing. With the addition of Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC, the yield stress value of the mixed solution increased by three orders of magnitude, resulting in the decrease of the fluidity of the ink, the gel state and the sol state of the ink kept at a suitable level, and the adaptability of the printing ink was greatly enhanced.

The curves in Fig. 7c, d were small amplitude temperature rise tests, which measured the curing performance of ink in oscillating mode. It can be seen from the graph that the gel point temperature of the PVA solution is 41.9 °C, and the addition of Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC increased the gel point temperature of the solution by 4.84 °C. The reason may be that the Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC contains a large number of hydroxyl groups, which make it have good water absorption and water retention properties, so the higher temperature is required in drying process. According to the Areniuz equation, the viscosity of fluid is closely related to temperature. With the increase of temperature, the damping factor of the two solutions first increased and then decreased, indicating that the viscosity of the solutions reached the maximum at 36.4 ℃ and 41.6 ℃, and then gradually decreased. Printing at this temperature will be very easy for forming, which has a good guiding significance for industrial printing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-counterfeiting application of Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC composites ink
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the superior rheological behavior mentioned above, we demonstrated the working performance of Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC composites ink in practical applications, where the Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC-PVA were added to waterborne ink. Therefore, this invisible ink can be used to write any substrates for security purpose. Firstly, we printed different shapes of graphic patterns on a paper, including two-dimensional code patterns, English letters, and art pictures, which was invisible in daylight (Fig. 8 a) but showed strong blue fluorescence under UV light as shown in Fig. 8. It is worth mentioning that the as-prepared fluorescent ink can be used in anti-counterfeiting QR code or bar code to store a lot of information, which can also be used in printing technology. The prepared Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC exhibited its luminescence properties in ink and showed bright and stable color under UV irradiation. Due to the narrow optical path of the 980 nm near-infrared laser, the near-infrared fluorescence image is not presented here. The printing effects of fluorescent inks on different substrates (Fig. S10) were explored in our follow-up work, including six substrates of linen, cotton cloth, cardboard, linen canvas, packaging wood boards, and polystyrene plastic boards, it performs very remarkable. Hence, this fluorescent ink can be widely used in information transmission, anti-counterfeiting, and many other fields due to its good fluorescent anti-counterfeiting performance.

The research developed and investigated the performance of an Er and Yb doped CQDs, which is a NFC supported CQDs capable of both traditional photoluminescence and up-conversion luminescence. In view of their desirable photoluminescence behavior, we combined the Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC composites with NFC suspensions to prepared waterborne ink, which emits 450 nm blue fluorescence under 370 nm excitation, while doped quantum dots emit 550 nm green fluorescence under 980 nm near infrared radiation, achieving dual anti-counterfeiting effect. In addition, the thixotropy and yield stress of the waterborne ink increased obviously after adding NFC, so this invisible ink can be used to write on any substrates for security purpose. This study provides experience for NFC as an ingredient to improve the performance of anti-counterfeiting ink. Moreover, UV-NIR dual-exciation anti-counterfeiting method can also be used in the future printing and packaging industry.

Pengfei Li: Methodology, Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Data curation, Software, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Kefu Chen: Writing - review & editing, Supervision.
